events.title: NIF Achieves Fusion Ignition for First Time
events.detail: On December 5, 2022, the National Ignition Facility (NIF) at Lawrence Livermore National Laboratory achieved fusion ignition — producing 3.15 MJ of fusion energy from 2.05 MJ of laser energy delivered to the target. This marks the first time in history that a fusion reaction produced more energy than the laser input, fulfilling a 60-year scientific goal. NIF Director Kim Budil called it "a triumph of science, engineering, and most of all, people." Note: the facility consumed ~300 MJ total to generate the laser pulse, so wall-plug efficiency remains ~1%.

sources.name: Lawrence Livermore National Laboratory (LLNL) official announcement

events.title: Helion Energy Signs First-Ever Fusion Power Purchase Agreement with Microsoft
events.detail: On May 10, 2023, Helion Energy announced a power purchase agreement (PPA) with Microsoft — the world's first commercial fusion energy contract. Helion committed to deliver at least 50 MW of electricity starting in 2028. The deal includes financial penalties if the deadline is missed. Helion's approach uses a field-reversed configuration (FRC) plasma that directly induces current in surrounding coils, bypassing traditional steam turbines.

events.title: JT-60SA Achieves First Plasma — World's Largest Operational Tokamak
events.detail: On December 1, 2023, the JT-60SA tokamak at the National Institutes for Quantum Science and Technology (QST) in Naka, Japan achieved first plasma, becoming the world's largest operational superconducting tokamak. A Japan-EU joint project under the Broader Approach agreement, JT-60SA has a plasma volume of 135 m³ and is designed to inform ITER and DEMO operations. First plasma was achieved on October 23, 2023 during commissioning and formally declared on December 1.

events.title: NIF Achieves Second Ignition with 3.88 MJ Yield
events.detail: On July 30, 2023, NIF conducted its second confirmed ignition shot, achieving 3.88 MJ of fusion energy output — a 23% increase over the December 2022 record. This demonstrated reproducibility of the ignition result and confirmed the December 2022 milestone was not a one-off anomaly. Subsequent shots in October 2023 (4th ignition, 3.4 MJ) further validated the results.

events.title: ITER Announces Major Schedule and Cost Revision
events.detail: ITER Organization, under new Director-General Pietro Barabaschi, disclosed that first plasma — originally scheduled for 2025 — must be delayed to 2033 (Baseline 2024 schedule). Full deuterium-tritium burning plasma operations are now targeted for 2039. The revision followed discovery of dimensional deviations in vacuum vessel sectors and stress corrosion cracks in thermal shields. Total cost estimate increased by €5.2 billion, bringing total to over €20 billion. Barabaschi described the new baseline as "realistic and achievable."

events.title: China's EAST Tokamak Sets 403-Second Plasma Duration Record
events.detail: China's Experimental Advanced Superconducting Tokamak (EAST) at the Institute of Plasma Physics in Hefei set a world record for sustained high-temperature plasma duration — 403 seconds at over 100 million degrees Celsius. This surpassed the previous EAST record of ~101 seconds (2022). The achievement is part of China's strategy to lead global fusion research as a "strategic frontier" priority under the 14th Five-Year Plan.

events.title: Commonwealth Fusion Systems Breaks Ground on SPARC Site in Devens, MA
events.detail: Commonwealth Fusion Systems (CFS), an MIT spinoff, began site preparation and construction work in Devens, Massachusetts for SPARC — its demonstration tokamak. SPARC is designed with a major radius of 1.85 m, magnetic field of 12.2 Tesla, and aims to demonstrate net energy gain (Q > 2) by the late 2020s. The compact high-field design is made possible by CFS's proprietary high-temperature superconducting (HTS) REBCO magnets, which achieved 20 Tesla in a September 2021 world-record test.

events.title: Wendelstein 7-X Stellarator Sets Energy Confinement Record
events.detail: Germany's Wendelstein 7-X (W7-X) stellarator at the Max-Planck-Institut für Plasmaphysik in Greifswald achieved a new record for energy stored in a stellarator plasma. W7-X, the world's largest stellarator, is designed to demonstrate that stellarators — which use twisted magnetic coils rather than plasma current for confinement — can achieve sustained, stable plasmas. Unlike tokamaks, stellarators do not require a plasma current that can become unstable, potentially enabling steady-state operation.

events.title: Global Private Fusion Investment Reaches $6.21 Billion — FIA Report
events.detail: The Fusion Industry Association (FIA) 2023 Global Fusion Industry Report documented that cumulative private investment in fusion energy had reached $6.21 billion, up from $4.8 billion in 2022. The report tracked 43 private fusion companies across 12 countries, with 25 based in the United States. 35 of 45 companies surveyed forecast commercially viable pilot plants between 2030 and 2035.

sources.name: Fusion Industry Association — 2023 Global Fusion Industry Report

events.title: NIF Sets New Record: 5.2 MJ Fusion Yield
events.detail: In February 2024, the National Ignition Facility achieved a new record fusion energy yield of 5.2 MJ from a 2.2 MJ laser input shot — a target gain of approximately 2.3×. This represents a 65% increase over the December 2022 ignition milestone and demonstrates that NIF can repeatedly achieve ignition with improving yields. The result was published in peer-reviewed literature and confirmed by independent analysis.

events.title: KSTAR Sustains 100 Million°C Plasma for 48 Seconds — New World Record
events.detail: South Korea's KSTAR (Korea Superconducting Tokamak Advanced Research) device sustained a plasma at 100 million degrees Celsius for 48 seconds during its 2023–2024 experimental campaign, announced April 2024. This broke the previous KSTAR record of 30 seconds (2021) and was enabled by an upgrade to tungsten divertors (replacing carbon) completed in 2023. The Korea Institute of Fusion Energy (KFE) aims to reach 300 seconds by 2026.

events.title: ITER Council Approves €5.2 Billion Budget Increase and Revised Schedule
events.detail: In July 2024, the ITER Council formally approved the Baseline 2024 — the latest revised schedule and budget for the project. The revision confirmed first plasma in 2033 and deuterium-tritium burning plasma in 2039. An additional €5.2 billion was added to the project budget, bringing the total estimated cost to approximately €20.4 billion. The revision was necessitated by manufacturing defects in vacuum vessel sectors and thermal shield components discovered during assembly.

events.title: Pacific Fusion Emerges from Stealth with $900M Series A
events.detail: In November 2024, Pacific Fusion — a previously stealth-mode inertial confinement fusion company — announced a $900 million Series A funding round, one of the largest in fusion industry history. The company focuses on pulsed fusion power using high-explosive-driven implosions. The funding represents continued strong investor confidence in fusion energy despite the long timelines to commercialization.

events.title: CFS Completes First SPARC Toroidal Field Magnet
events.detail: Commonwealth Fusion Systems completed the first of 18 toroidal field (TF) magnets for the SPARC tokamak at its Devens, Massachusetts facility. Each TF magnet uses high-temperature superconducting REBCO tape wound into a D-shape and must be tested at cryogenic temperatures. The completion of the first magnet represented a major manufacturing milestone in SPARC construction, with the full set of 18 required for tokamak assembly.

events.title: UK STEP Programme Enters Phase 2 Engineering Design
events.detail: The UK's Spherical Tokamak for Energy Production (STEP) programme at the West Burton A site in Nottinghamshire entered Phase 2 — detailed engineering design — in 2024. STEP is designed to generate ~100 MWe net electricity using a spherical tokamak configuration, with operations expected in the 2040s. Phase 2 runs from 2025–2032 and involves procurement of engineering and construction partners.

events.title: Helion Completes Construction of Polaris — 7th Fusion Prototype
events.detail: Helion Energy completed construction of Polaris, its 7th and most advanced fusion prototype, in late 2024. Polaris uses a field-reversed configuration (FRC) approach in which two magnetized plasma rings are accelerated toward each other at ~1 million mph and compressed to achieve fusion conditions. Polaris is designed to demonstrate net electricity production — a prerequisite for the 2028 Microsoft power delivery target. The device began operations forming the largest FRC plasmas in Helion history.

events.title: TAE Technologies Reports Breakthrough Performance on Norman Machine
events.detail: TAE Technologies reported that its Norman (Norm) research machine achieved its highest plasma performance levels, exceeding all prior targets. TAE's approach — using a field-reversed configuration fueled with hydrogen and boron (p-B11, aneutronic fusion) — aims to produce energy without high-energy neutrons, dramatically reducing radiation shielding requirements. The Norm machine demonstrated plasma parameters approaching the conditions required for p-B11 fusion, despite the more challenging physics compared to deuterium-tritium approaches.

events.title: China's EAST Achieves 1,066-Second Plasma — World Record
events.detail: On January 20, 2025, China's EAST (Experimental Advanced Superconducting Tokamak) tokamak at the Institute of Plasma Physics (ASIPP) in Hefei achieved a sustained plasma duration of 1,066 seconds (nearly 18 minutes) at over 100 million degrees Celsius — more than doubling its previous record of 403 seconds. The achievement used new plasma diagnostics and doubled heating system power. French tokamak WEST later set a competing record of 1,337 seconds (22+ minutes) in February 2025.

events.title: French WEST Tokamak Breaks EAST Record with 1,337-Second Plasma
events.detail: France's WEST (Tungsten (W) Environment in Steady-state Tokamak) device at CEA's IRFM facility in Cadarache achieved a world record plasma duration of 1,337 seconds (~22 minutes) in February 2025, surpassing the 1,066-second record set by China's EAST just weeks earlier. WEST uses an all-tungsten first wall and is designed to test plasma-wall interactions for ITER. The record demonstrates Europe's continued competitive position in long-pulse plasma research.

sources.name: CEA (Commissariat à l'énergie atomique) — official press release

events.title: Helion Raises $425M Series F with SoftBank; Valued at $5.4 Billion
events.detail: In January 2025, Helion Energy announced a $425 million Series F funding round with SoftBank Vision Fund 2 as a lead investor. The round valued Helion at $5.4 billion, making it one of the most highly valued private fusion companies. The funding will support continued development of the Polaris prototype and progress toward the 2028 Microsoft power delivery target. Sam Altman, an early Helion backer, again participated.

events.title: UK Announces £410M in Record Fusion Funding for 2025–2026
events.detail: On January 16, 2025, the UK government announced £410 million in fusion research funding — the highest annual allocation in UK fusion history. The funding covers STEP programme progression, the Fusion Futures skills pipeline, JET decommissioning research, and UKAEA operational support. A subsequent commitment of £2.5 billion over the following years was announced, cementing the UK's position as a major global fusion investor and supporting its ambition to become a world leader in commercial fusion energy.

events.title: TAE Technologies Raises $150M; Total Funding Exceeds $1.3 Billion
events.detail: In June 2025, TAE Technologies raised $150 million in a new funding round, with investors including Google, Chevron Technology Ventures, and NEA. TAE's total equity capital raised passed $1.3 billion, making it one of the best-funded alternative fusion approaches globally. TAE's Norman machine continues to advance toward conditions required for hydrogen-boron (p-B11) aneutronic fusion — a pathway that would produce no high-energy neutrons.

events.title: Helion Polaris Achieves 150 Million°C in Deuterium-Tritium Fusion
events.detail: On February 13, 2026, Helion Energy announced that its Polaris prototype had achieved a plasma temperature of 150 million degrees Celsius — significantly exceeding the 100 million degree threshold — using deuterium-tritium (D-T) fuel. This represents the highest temperature achieved by Helion to date and a critical step toward demonstrating net electricity production. Helion's FRC approach directly converts fusion energy to electricity via induced magnetic fields, eliminating the need for steam turbines.

events.title: CFS Completes First SPARC Toroidal Field Magnet for Full Assembly
events.detail: In January 2026, Commonwealth Fusion Systems completed and tested the first production toroidal field magnet for SPARC assembly at its Devens, Massachusetts facility. The magnet — made from high-temperature superconducting REBCO tape — must achieve 12.2 Tesla in operation. CFS requires 18 such magnets for SPARC. The SPARC tokamak is designed to achieve fusion energy gain Q > 2, making it the first tokamak to demonstrate net fusion energy gain if successful.

events.title: CFS Raises $863M Series B2 — Nvidia and Google Among Investors
events.detail: In August 2025, Commonwealth Fusion Systems announced an $863 million Series B2 funding round, bringing total capital raised to nearly $3 billion. New investors included Nvidia, Google, Breakthrough Energy Ventures, Gates Frontier, Khosla Ventures, and Eni. CFS is now the world's best-funded private fusion company. The funds support construction of SPARC at Devens, MA and advance planning for the ARC commercial fusion power plant.

events.title: US DOE FY2025 Fusion Budget Reaches $790M for Fusion Energy Sciences
events.detail: The US Department of Energy's Office of Fusion Energy Sciences (FES) received $790 million in FY2025 appropriations, supporting fusion research across national labs, universities, and the US contribution to ITER (~$200M). Separately, DOE's Inertial Confinement Fusion program received ~$683M, the majority going to NIF at Lawrence Livermore. Total federal fusion investment in FY2025 exceeded $1.4 billion, the highest in US history.

events.title: Westinghouse Awarded €168M ITER Vacuum Vessel Assembly Contract
events.detail: Westinghouse Electric Company was awarded a €168 million ($180M USD) contract for final assembly operations on ITER's vacuum vessel sectors. The contract covers machining and welding of the nine 400-tonne D-shaped steel sectors that form the toroidal vacuum vessel. Earlier quality inspections had found dimensional deviations and stress corrosion cracking in thermal shields, contributing to the schedule delay. ITER requires millimeter-precision positioning for the automated welding tools.

events.title: Cumulative Private Fusion Investment Surpasses $9.8 Billion — FIA
events.detail: The Fusion Industry Association's 2025 data showed cumulative private investment in fusion energy had exceeded $9.8 billion — representing a 5× increase since 2021. The rolling 12-month investment rate of $2.64 billion (to July 2025) was the highest annual rate since 2022, signaling renewed investor confidence. The number of private fusion companies tracked reached 43+ across 12 countries.

events.title: Wendelstein 7-X Achieves Record Energy Stored in Stellarator Plasma
events.detail: Germany's Wendelstein 7-X (W7-X) stellarator at Max-Planck-Institut für Plasmaphysik in Greifswald set a new world record for energy content in a stellarator plasma in its 2023–2024 experimental campaign. W7-X's key advantage over tokamaks is its inherently steady-state design — no plasma current means no disruptive instabilities. The record validated that optimized stellarator geometry (computed via years of numerical optimization) can achieve confinement comparable to tokamaks, advancing the case for stellarators as a commercial fusion route.

events.title: KSTAR Announces Upgraded Target: 300 Seconds at 100M°C by 2026
events.detail: Following its 48-second plasma record in early 2024, the Korea Institute of Fusion Energy (KFE) formally announced a 300-second sustained plasma target at 100 million degrees Celsius as its next major milestone, to be achieved by 2026. The upgraded tungsten divertor (replacing the previous carbon system) enables much longer plasma durations by better handling the heat exhaust. A 300-second record would represent a 6× improvement and bring KSTAR plasma parameters closer to the continuous operation needed for a power plant.

sources.name: Korea Institute of Fusion Energy (KFE) — official statement
